real_woman.name: Ada Lovelace
real_woman.achievement: Wrote the first computer program in history, over 100 years before modern computers existed

story.title: Lily and the Lady Who Built the Future

pages.text: Lily loved building days at school more than anything. She loved snapping blocks together, stacking towers tall enough to wobble, and figuring out how to make bridges that didn't fall down. But today, when she reached for the big bucket of building bricks, a boy named Marcus pulled it away. "Building stuff is for boys," he said, like he was reading a fact from a textbook. A few other kids nodded, and Lily felt her hands drop to her sides. She looked around the room and noticed that all the other girls had drifted to the art table. Maybe Marcus was right. Maybe girls weren't supposed to build things. She sat down quietly and picked up a crayon, but her fingers itched for the blocks.
pages.illustration_description: A bright, cheerful classroom scene. In the foreground, a 6-year-old girl (Lily) sits at an art table holding a crayon, but her eyes are looking longingly across the room toward a building station where several children play with colorful construction bricks and wooden blocks. Her expression is wistful, not sad — she clearly wants to be building. The classroom is warm and inviting with sunlight streaming through windows, student artwork on the walls, and a cozy reading corner in the background. The crayon in her hand rests idle on the paper. The mood is gentle but carries a quiet ache of longing.

pages.text: After school, Lily walked home with her grandmother, kicking a pebble along the sidewalk. They passed the new library being built on Maple Street, and Lily stopped. Up on the scaffolding, a woman in a bright yellow hard hat was reading blueprints — giant paper maps of the building. She pointed and called out directions, and workers moved steel beams exactly where she said. "Grandma," Lily whispered, tugging her grandmother's sleeve. "She's building the whole library." Her grandmother smiled a knowing smile. "Of course she is, sweetheart. Women have built bridges, buildings, computers, and entire companies." The word "computers" buzzed in Lily's ear like a little spark. "Computers?" she asked. "Who built computers?" Her grandmother's eyes twinkled. "Come with me. I have something extraordinary to show you."
pages.illustration_description: A late afternoon street scene showing Lily and her grandmother standing on a sidewalk, looking up at a construction site for a new library. A woman in a bright yellow hard hat and safety vest stands confidently on scaffolding, holding large blueprint papers and pointing upward with authority. Steel beams and partially built walls frame the background. The sky is a warm golden-orange from the afternoon sun. Lily is looking up with wide, amazed eyes, one hand tugging on her grandmother's sleeve. The grandmother is a warm, gentle figure with kind eyes and a knowing smile. The mood is one of wonder and discovery — a crack of light breaking through.

pages.text: Grandma led Lily to the attic, where old trunks sat under dusty beams of light. She opened a wooden chest and pulled out a large, golden-edged book titled "Remarkable Minds." She turned to a page with a painting of a woman in a sweeping Victorian dress, standing beside an enormous machine made of gears, wheels, and brass levers. "This is Ada Lovelace," Grandma said softly, "and she changed the world." Lily leaned closer. The machine in the picture looked like a giant metal puzzle. "Everyone else saw this machine as just a big calculator — something that could only do math," Grandma continued. "But Ada saw something magical. She saw that it could create music, art, and ideas. She wrote the very first set of instructions for it, and computing was born." Lily touched the edge of the painting. She could almost hear the gears turning.
pages.illustration_description: A cozy, magical attic scene bathed in warm golden light filtering through a small round window. Lily and her grandmother sit together on the wooden floor beside an open antique trunk. The grandmother holds open a large, beautiful book with gilded edges. On the open page, there is a vivid illustration of Ada Lovelace in an elegant Victorian-era dress, standing beside Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine — a fantastical machine of brass gears, wheels, cogs, and levers. Soft, sparkly golden dust motes float in the air, giving the scene a magical quality. Lily leans in close, her face illuminated by the book as if it's glowing. Around the painted machine on the page, faint magical swirls suggest music notes, tiny stars, and artistic patterns emerging — hinting at Ada's vision. The mood is enchanting, intimate, and full of wonder.

pages.text: Grandma turned the page, and the story of Ada unfolded like a fairy tale. Over one hundred years before anyone had a laptop or a phone or even a television, Ada Lovelace sat at a wooden desk with a quill pen and a brilliant idea. Most people in her time thought girls should only learn music and manners, not math and machines. But Ada's mind was like a sky full of fireworks — too bright and too wild to be kept small. She studied numbers the way poets study words, finding beauty and rhythm hidden inside them. When a famous inventor showed her his calculating machine, everyone else shrugged and said, "It does sums." But Ada saw further than anyone. She wrote the world's first computer program — a detailed set of instructions that told the machine what to do — and she imagined that one day, machines like it could compose symphonies and paint pictures. People laughed. But Ada was right.
pages.illustration_description: A fairy-tale style illustration split into flowing, dreamlike scenes. In the center, Ada Lovelace sits at an elegant wooden desk with a quill pen, writing by candlelight, her expression focused and joyful. She wears a beautiful Victorian dress with intricate details. Around her, the background swirls into imaginative visions of the future she predicted: musical notes floating from a gear-driven machine, colorful paintings emerging from mechanical arms, and faint ghostly outlines of modern laptops and tablets glowing softly in the distance like prophecies. Below her desk, tiny figures of skeptical men in top hats look confused or dismissive, but Ada sits above them, radiant and confident. The color palette is rich — deep purples, midnight blues, and brilliant golds — giving the page the feeling of a magical legend. Stars and mathematical symbols drift through the air like enchanted dust.

pages.text: The next morning, Lily walked into school with something new glowing inside her chest — a warm, steady feeling, like a small engine humming. When the building blocks came out, she didn't wait for permission. She walked straight to the brick station, sat down, and started building. Marcus looked over. "What are you making?" he asked. "A computer," Lily said, snapping two blocks together with a satisfying click. "Like the one Ada Lovelace imagined — except mine is going to have wings." She built it tall, with gears made of blue blocks and wide red wings stretching out from each side. Two other girls pulled up chairs and started adding pieces. By the end of the morning, their flying computer had a name — "The Ada" — and it was the tallest thing in the room. Lily smiled at it. Girls could build things, all right. Girls could build the future.
pages.illustration_description: A joyful, bright classroom scene bursting with energy and color. Lily stands proudly at the building station, her hands placing the final block on top of an elaborate, imaginative construction made of colorful blocks — a fantastical flying computer with wide red wings, blue gear-like structures, and creative details. Two other girls sit beside her, happily adding their own pieces to the creation. A small handwritten sign on the table reads 'The Ada.' Other classmates, including Marcus, look on with curiosity and admiration. The morning sunlight floods the room with warm, golden light. Lily's posture is confident and tall, her face beaming with pride and happiness. The mood is triumphant, warm, and full of possibility — a new day, a new belief.

story.real_woman_achievement: Ada Lovelace wrote the world's first computer program in the 1840s, over a hundred years before modern computers existed, and envisioned that machines could go beyond math to create music and art.
discussion_prompts: Has anyone ever told you that you couldn't do something because of who you are? How did that make you feel?
Why do you think Ada Lovelace could see things in the machine that other people couldn't see?
If you could build anything in the whole world — anything at all — what would you build and why?
story.activity_suggestion: Build your own 'Ada Machine' using blocks, cardboard boxes, recycled materials, or LEGO bricks. Imagine a machine that could do something magical — play music, tell stories, or fly — and build it together. Give it a name and write down three instructions for what it should do, just like Ada wrote the first computer program.

issues: Page 1 shows all other girls drifting to the art table, which could inadvertently suggest girls naturally prefer art over building
The phrase 'Girls could build things, all right' uses slightly defensive language that acknowledges the stereotype's validity as something needing proof
strengths: Lily is described through her actions and interests, not appearance
Ada Lovelace's story is woven naturally into the narrative through the grandmother's storytelling
The story shows systemic change (other girls join) rather than just individual triumph
Language is age-appropriate with vivid, child-friendly metaphors ('sky full of fireworks', 'small engine humming')
Historical context about Ada facing gender barriers is included without being heavy-handed
Marcus is not villainized - he simply asks what she's making, showing potential for learning
The ending is realistic (building with blocks) while still empowering
Lily shows agency throughout - she makes choices, asks questions, and takes action
The grandmother character provides mentorship without rescuing Lily
